phase_metadata.description:
Master the art of writing resilient Java programs by handling
runtime errors, managing resources, and creating custom error types.

learning_objectives:
Understand the Throwable
hierarchy and the difference between Error and Exception

Distinguish between
Checked and Unchecked exceptions

Implement try-catch-finally blocks for
robust error management

Use 'throw' to trigger exceptions and 'throws' to
delegate them
Utilize try-with-resources for automatic resource management
Create and implement custom user-defined exceptions
introduction:
In the real world,
things go wrong: files are missing, network connections drop, or users enter
invalid data. If these situations aren't handled, a Java program will 'crash'
(terminate abruptly). Exception Handling is Java's mechanism to intercept these
errors, handle them gracefully, and allow the program to continue running or
shut down safely.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
An Exception is an
unwanted or unexpected event that occurs during the execution of a program at
runtime. Exception Handling is a way to manage these errors without stopping the
JVM.
concept_explanation.why_do_we_need_it:
Without it, an error in one line of code stops the
entire application. With it, we can provide fallback logic, log the error, and
keep the application alive.
concept_explanation.exception_hierarchy:
All exceptions stem from
the 'Throwable' class. It branches into 'Error' (serious JVM problems like
OutOfMemory) and 'Exception' (programmatic issues). 'Exception' is further
divided into Checked (compile-time) and Unchecked (runtime) exceptions.
concept_explanation.checked_vs_unchecked:
Checked exceptions (e.g., IOException) must be handled
or declared at compile time. Unchecked exceptions (e.g., NullPointerException)
occur at runtime due to logic flaws and aren't forced by the compiler.
concept_explanation.try_with_resources:
Introduced in Java 7, this feature ensures that resources
like file streams or database connections are closed automatically at the end of
the block, even if an exception occurs.

syntax_breakdown.try:
Defines a block of code to be tested for errors
while it is being executed.
syntax_breakdown.catch:
Defines a block of code to be executed
if an error occurs in the try block.
syntax_breakdown.finally:
A block that executes
regardless of whether an exception was caught or not. Used for cleanup.
syntax_breakdown.throw:
Used to explicitly throw an exception from a method or block of
code.
syntax_breakdown.throws:
Used in a method signature to indicate that this method might
throw specific exceptions to its caller.
syntax_breakdown.multi_catch:
Catching multiple
exceptions in one block: catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... }

rules_and_key_points:
A try block must be followed by at least one catch
block or a finally block.

The catch block for a subclass exception must
appear before the catch block for a superclass exception.

The finally block
always executes unless the JVM exits during the try/catch (e.g.,
System.exit()).

Checked exceptions are checked at compile-time; Unchecked
exceptions are not.

The 'throws' keyword is used for exception delegation
(ducking the exception).

A method can throw any number of exceptions
separated by commas in the signature.

Custom exceptions should extend the
Exception class (checked) or RuntimeException (unchecked).

Try-with-resources
only works with classes that implement the AutoCloseable interface.

Once an
exception is thrown, the normal flow of the program stops and the JVM searches
for a matching catch block.

Never leave a catch block empty (swallowing
exceptions); at least log the error.

code_example_1.title:
Basic
Try-Catch-Finally
code_example_1.description:
Demonstrating how to handle a potential
division by zero and ensure cleanup code runs.

code_example_2.title: Custom Exception and Try-With-Resources
code_example_2.description:
Creating a custom 'InvalidAgeException' and using
try-with-resources with a custom AutoCloseable class.
code_example_2.expected_output:
Error: You cannot divide by zero!
Cleanup: This always
runs.
Program continues...
Using resource...
Resource closed
automatically.
Caught Custom Error: Access denied - Age must be 18+
code_example_2.step_by_step_dry_run:
1. In BasicException, '10 / 0' triggers an
ArithmeticException object.
2. The JVM looks for a matching catch. It finds
'catch (ArithmeticException e)'.
3. The error message is printed.
4. The
finally block executes regardless of the error.
5. In AdvancedException, the
try-with-resources block starts and 'res' is initialized.
6. 'checkAge(15)' is
called. Since 15 < 18, it 'throws' an InvalidAgeException.
7. Before jumping to
the catch block, the try-with-resources automatically calls 'res.close()'.
8.
The catch block for InvalidAgeException then executes.
code_example_2.memory_visualization:
When an exception occurs, the JVM creates an Exception Object on the Heap. This
object contains the stack trace (a snapshot of the method calls). The JVM then
stops the current execution and searches the Stack for a matching 'catch'
handler. If it reaches the bottom of the stack (main) without finding one, the
default exception handler prints the stack trace and terminates the thread.
real_world_use_cases:
Database Connectivity: Handling cases where the
database server is offline.

User Input: Validating that a user enters a
number where a string is expected.

File Processing: Gracefully handling 'File
Not Found' errors.

Network Communication: Managing timeouts when a website is
slow to respond.
common_mistakes:
Catching 'Exception' or 'Throwable'
as the first catch block (this masks more specific errors).

Using Exception
handling for normal control flow (it is computationally expensive).

Forgetting to close resources in the finally block (use try-with-resources
instead).
Not logging the stack trace, making debugging impossible later.

Throwing a checked exception but forgetting to add 'throws' to the method
signature.
best_practices:
Catch specific exceptions instead of the
generic Exception class.

Throw early, catch late (throw the error where it
happens, handle it where you can make a decision).

Always use
try-with-resources for streams and database connections.

Include meaningful
messages in your exceptions.

Use custom exceptions to make your API more
readable and business-oriented.

faq.question:
Can we have a try
block without a catch block?
faq.answer:
Yes, but only if there is a finally
block. However, if an exception occurs, it will not be handled in that method.

faq.question: What is the difference between final, finally, and finalize?
faq.answer:
'final' is a modifier for variables/classes/methods. 'finally' is a
block for exception cleanup. 'finalize()' is a method used by the Garbage
Collector before an object is destroyed (deprecated).

faq.question:
Does
finally execute if I use System.exit(0)?
faq.answer:
No. System.exit(0)
terminates the JVM immediately, so the finally block will not execute.

code_example_2.summary:
Phase 9 taught us how to handle unexpected errors in Java. We
explored the try-catch-finally structure, learned the difference between checked
and unchecked exceptions, and saw how modern Java uses try-with-resources to
manage resources efficiently. We also learned how to define our own exception
types for specific business logic.
key_takeaways: Checked exceptions are enforced by the compiler.

Try-with-resources is the best way to handle
auto-closable objects.
code_example_2.preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 10, we will dive into the Java
Collections Framework, learning how to store and manipulate groups of objects
using Lists, Sets, and Maps.

questions.question:
What is the base class for all classes that can be
thrown in Java?
questions.answer:
The 'java.lang.Throwable' class is the superclass
of all errors and exceptions in the Java language.

questions.question:
Explain the 'Exception Propagation'
mechanism.
questions.answer:
If an exception is not caught in the method where it
occurred, it 'propagates' or drops down to the previous method in the call
stack. This continues until it is caught or reaches the main method, where the
JVM terminates the program if unhandled.

questions.question:
Provide a code snippet showing how to
catch both 'NullPointerException' and 'ArithmeticException' in a single catch
block.

Create a custom
exception 'InsufficientFundsException' and a method 'withdraw(int amount)' that
throws it if the amount is greater than 1000.
questions.answer:
The code will not compile. ArithmeticException is a subclass of
Exception. Since catch blocks are checked in order, the Exception block will
catch everything, making the ArithmeticException block 'unreachable code'.

questions.question:
Why would a
'try-with-resources' block fail to compile if used with a 'String' object inside
the parentheses?
questions.answer:
Try-with-resources requires the object declared
inside the parentheses to implement the 'java.lang.AutoCloseable' interface. The
'String' class does not implement this interface, so it cannot be used there.

questions.question:
Write a method
that takes a String, converts it to an Integer using Integer.parseInt(), and
catches the potential NumberFormatException.

Implement a try-finally block where the try
block returns a value, and explain if the finally block still executes.

code.question:
Which block is used to write cleanup code
like closing a database connection?

Which
keyword is used in a method signature to declare an exception?

Which exception is thrown by the JVM when an array is accessed with
an invalid index?

code_example_1.question:
Can a catch block exist
without a try block?

options.C:
Only if it is
in an interface

code_example_1.question:
Best Approach: How should you handle a Checked Exception if you cannot resolve
it locally in the method?

options.A:
Wrap it in a try-catch and do
nothing
options.B: Declare it using the 'throws' keyword in the method signature
options.C: Print the stack trace and return null
